news_summary: Recent scientific discoveries reveal promising signs of ocean recovery and unique biodiversity, while innovative urban pocket gardens and emerging solar technologies offer sustainable environmental benefits.
news_summary_fr: Des découvertes scientifiques récentes révèlent des signes encourageants de régénération des océans et d'une biodiversité unique, tandis que les jardins urbains innovants et les nouvelles technologies solaires apportent des avantages environnementaux durables.
news_summary_es: Los recientes descubrimientos científicos revelan signos prometedores de recuperación de los océanos y una biodiversidad única, mientras que los innovadores jardines urbanos de pequeño tamaño y las tecnologías solares emergentes ofrecen beneficios medioambientales sostenibles.

articles.title: Scientists open 40-year-old salmon and find a surprising sign of ocean recovery
articles.summary: Old canned salmon turned out to be a time capsule of ocean health. Researchers found that rising levels of tiny parasitic worms in some salmon species suggest stronger, more complete marine food webs. Because these parasites depend on multiple hosts—including marine mammals—their increase may reflect ecosystem recovery over decades. What looks unappetizing may actually be a sign of a healthier ocean.
articles.content:
Alaska's waters support one of the world's most important salmon fisheries, sustained by complex marine food webs. Scientists are eager to understand how these systems are changing as the climate shifts. The challenge is that reliable samples from decades ago are hard to find.

"We have to really open our minds and get creative about what can act as an ecological data source," said Natalie Mastick, currently a postdoctoral researcher at the Peabody Museum of Natural History at Yale University.

Scientists Turn to an Unusual Source: Decades-Old Salmon

While working on her doctorate at the University of Washington in Seattle, Mastick took an unconventional approach. Instead of relying on modern samples, she examined old cans of salmon.

These cans contained fillets from four species collected over a 42-year span in the Gulf of Alaska and Bristol Bay. Mastick and her team opened 178 cans and carefully dissected the preserved fish, counting tiny parasitic worms known as anisakids embedded in the flesh.

Although these parasites had been killed during the canning process and would not harm consumers, they hold valuable scientific information.

Why "Sushi Worms" Can Signal a Healthy Ecosystem

At first glance, finding worms in fish may seem alarming. But scientists say the opposite can be true.

"Everyone assumes that worms in your salmon is a sign that things have gone awry," said Chelsea Wood, a UW associate professor of aquatic and fishery sciences. "But the anisakid life cycle integrates many components of the food web. I see their presence as a signal that the fish on your plate came from a healthy ecosystem."

Because these parasites depend on multiple species to survive, their presence can reflect the overall strength of the marine ecosystem.

Rising Parasite Levels Reveal Long-Term Ocean Trends

The team's findings, published in Ecology & Evolution, showed that anisakid levels increased in chum and pink salmon between 1979 and 2021. In coho and sockeye salmon, parasite levels remained steady.

"Anisakids have a complex life cycle that requires many types of hosts," said Mastick, who is lead author on the paper. "Seeing their numbers rise over time, as we did with pink and chum salmon, indicates that these parasites were able to find all the right hosts and reproduce. That could indicate a stable or recovering ecosystem, with enough of the right hosts for anisakids."

How Parasites Track the Entire Marine Food Web

Anisakids begin life as free-floating organisms in the ocean. They enter the food chain when small creatures like krill consume them. As predators eat those infected animals, the parasites move up the food web.

For example, krill may be eaten by small fish, which are then eaten by larger fish such as salmon. Eventually, the parasites reach marine mammals, where they reproduce. Their eggs are released back into the ocean, restarting the cycle.

"If a host is not present -- marine mammals, for example -- anisakids can't complete their life cycle and their numbers will drop," said Wood, who is senior author on the paper.

Are These Parasites Dangerous to People?

Humans are not part of the anisakid life cycle. Eating properly cooked fish poses little risk because the worms are already dead.

However, anisakids, also called "sushi worms" or "sushi parasites," can cause illness if consumed alive in raw or undercooked seafood. Symptoms may resemble food poisoning or, in rare cases, a condition known as anisakiasis.

Where Did the Salmon Samples Come From?

The canned salmon used in the study came from the Seafood Products Association, a Seattle-based trade group. The organization had preserved these cans over many years for quality control but eventually no longer needed them.

Mastick and co-author Rachel Welicky, an assistant professor at Neumann University in Pennsylvania, tested different techniques to analyze the samples. The worms, about a centimeter (0.4 inches) long, often curl up inside the fish muscle. By gently pulling apart the fillets with forceps and using a dissecting microscope, the team was able to count them accurately.

Why Are Parasite Levels Increasing?

There are several possible explanations for the rise in anisakid levels in some salmon species.

One key factor may be the Marine Mammal Protection Act of 1972, which helped populations of seals, sea lions, orcas, and other marine mammals recover after years of decline.

"Anisakids can only reproduce in the intestines of a marine mammal, so this could be a sign that, over our study period -- from 1979 to 2021 -- anisakid levels were rising because of more opportunities to reproduce," said Mastick.

Other contributing factors could include warming ocean temperatures or environmental improvements linked to the Clean Water Act.

Why Some Salmon Species Show No Change

The stable parasite levels observed in coho and sockeye salmon are more difficult to interpret.

There are many different anisakid species, each relying on its own combination of hosts. While the canning process preserved the outer structure of the worms, it destroyed the internal features scientists would need to identify specific species.

A New Way to Study the Ocean's Past

Researchers believe this method could be applied to other archived seafood, such as canned sardines, offering a new way to explore historical ecosystems.

They also hope their work encourages scientists to think creatively about untapped data sources.

"This study came about because people heard about our research through the grapevine," said Wood. "We can only get these insights into ecosystems of the past by networking and making the connections to discover untapped sources of historical data."

Funding and Contributors

The study included UW undergraduate Aspen Katla, along with Bruce Odegaard and Virginia Ng from the Seafood Products Association. Funding was provided by the U.S. National Science Foundation, the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation, the Washington Research Foundation, and the University of Washington.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery indicating long-term marine ecosystem recovery, evidenced by rising parasite levels in salmon that reflect stronger food webs and marine mammal population rebounds. This has broad environmental significance and offers a novel method for studying historical ocean health, providing hope and insight into ecosystem restoration.
articles.personality_title: Scientists use 40-year-old canned salmon to reveal ocean recovery
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Alaska’s salmon fisheries depend on complex ocean food webs that are changing with the climate. Scientists want to understand these changes but lack old samples to study.

**What happened** – A team led by researcher Natalie Mastick studied 178 cans of salmon collected from 1979 to 2021. They counted tiny parasitic worms called anisakids inside the fish. These parasites need several hosts, including marine mammals, to complete their life cycle.

**Impact** – The number of these parasites increased in some salmon species over 40 years, suggesting more marine mammals and stronger food webs. This means the ocean ecosystem may be recovering. The study shows that parasites, usually seen as bad, can actually provide clues about ocean health.

**What’s next step** – Researchers hope to apply this method to other archived seafood to learn more about past ocean conditions. This creative approach could help scientists study environmental changes over time.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Counting parasites in old canned salmon reveals that Alaska’s ocean ecosystems have been recovering for decades.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques utilisent du saumon en conserve vieux de 40 ans pour révéler la récupération des océans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les pêcheries de saumon en Alaska dépendent de réseaux alimentaires marins complexes qui évoluent avec le climat. Les scientifiques veulent comprendre ces changements, mais manquent d’échantillons anciens.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par la chercheuse Natalie Mastick a étudié 178 boîtes de saumon collectées entre 1979 et 2021. Ils ont compté de petits vers parasites appelés anisakidés dans les poissons. Ces parasites ont besoin de plusieurs hôtes, dont des mammifères marins, pour compléter leur cycle de vie.

**Impact** – Le nombre de ces parasites a augmenté chez certaines espèces de saumon sur 40 ans, ce qui suggère plus de mammifères marins et des réseaux alimentaires plus solides. Cela signifie que l’écosystème océanique pourrait se rétablir. L’étude montre que les parasites, habituellement vus comme négatifs, peuvent en fait donner des indices sur la santé des océans.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent appliquer cette méthode à d’autres produits de la mer conservés pour mieux comprendre les conditions passées des océans. Cette approche créative pourrait aider à étudier les changements environnementaux au fil du temps.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Compter les parasites dans du saumon en conserve ancien révèle que les écosystèmes marins de l’Alaska se rétablissent depuis des décennies.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos usan salmón enlatado de hace 40 años para revelar la recuperación del océano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las pesquerías de salmón en Alaska dependen de redes alimenticias marinas complejas que están cambiando con el clima. Los científicos quieren entender estos cambios pero carecen de muestras antiguas.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por la investigadora Natalie Mastick estudió 178 latas de salmón recogidas entre 1979 y 2021. Contaron pequeños gusanos parásitos llamados anisákidos dentro de los peces. Estos parásitos necesitan varios huéspedes, incluidos mamíferos marinos, para completar su ciclo de vida.

**Impacto** – La cantidad de estos parásitos aumentó en algunas especies de salmón durante 40 años, lo que sugiere más mamíferos marinos y redes alimenticias más fuertes. Esto indica que el ecosistema oceánico podría estar recuperándose. El estudio muestra que los parásitos, normalmente vistos como algo malo, pueden dar pistas sobre la salud del océano.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan aplicar este método a otros productos del mar archivados para aprender más sobre las condiciones pasadas del océano. Este enfoque creativo podría ayudar a estudiar cambios ambientales a lo largo del tiempo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Contar parásitos en salmón enlatado antiguo revela que los ecosistemas oceánicos de Alaska se han estado recuperando durante décadas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vintage open can of salmon fillets resting on a rustic wooden table by the sea, with small, delicate parasitic worms nestled visibly within the fish flesh, surrounded by symbolic silhouettes of thriving marine mammals like seals and orcas swimming gracefully in calm ocean waters under a soft, glowing sky.

articles.title: Pocket gardens: The tiny urban oases with surprisingly big benefits
articles.content:
It’s not just easy to miss, but often downright hard to notice. A simple patch of greenery in a city may seem like a blip in the concrete jungle, but it’s an extremely powerful way to solve a bunch of problems at once: Studies have shown that green spaces improve urbanites’ mental health, make summers more bearable, and prevent flooding by soaking up stormwater.

When these plots are planned — as opposed to letting vacant lots grow wild, which is valuable in its own right — they become extra powerful. You may have even enjoyed one without knowing it: the “pocket garden.” Tucked into spaces accessible to pedestrians, like sidewalks, hospital grounds, and campuses, they can be engineered to turn heat-absorbing concrete into air-cooling oases packed with vegetation and seating for people to escape the metropolitan bustle.

“This increasing prioritization of creating green spaces in unexpected spots and underutilized spaces in communities is not only going to be making our communities more resilient, it’s going to be making people healthier,” said Dan Lambe, chief executive of the nonprofit Arbor Day Foundation, which promotes urban forestry. “A little bit of green goes a long way.”

Pocket gardens aren’t gardens in the agriculturally productive sense, but ornamental grounds. (Though there’s nothing stopping a designer from adding a fruit tree or two.) Ideally, they’re host to native plant species, which bring several benefits. For one, they attract native pollinators like insects and birds, which get a source of food that powers them to go on and fertilize plants elsewhere, like crops in urban farms. And two, if the vegetation is adapted to a particular region or condition, it’s already used to the local climate — drought-tolerant varieties, for instance, won’t require as much water to survive. Furthermore, choosing native grasses that don’t need mowing can cut down on maintenance costs. And picking trees with big canopies will increase the amount of shade for people to use as refuge from the heat. (Sorry, palm trees, that means you’re disqualified.)

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Biodiversity — mixing tree species as opposed to planting 10 of the same kind — is key here. That attracts a broader range of pollinating animals, and builds resiliency into the system: If you only plant one variety of tree and a disease shows up, it can spread rapidly.

And speaking of disease, trees have an additional superpower in their ability to scrub urban air of the pollutants that contribute to respiratory problems. In addition, the vegetation of a pocket park releases water vapor, bringing down air temperatures. This mitigates what’s called the urban heat island effect, in which cities absorb the sun’s energy all day and slowly release it into the night. Combined, reduced air pollution and temperatures improve public health.

There’s also the harder-to-quantify bonus of people getting out of their cars and gathering in public spaces, no matter how diminutive. “It’s actually a transition toward the pedestrian — toward the person — and away from the vehicle,” said Eric Galipo, director of campus planning and urban design at the architecture firm FCA, which has integrated pocket gardens in their projects. (The photo at the top of this story is of one of the firm’s projects, at Newark Beth Israel Medical Center.) “We may not spend as much time together as a society as we used to, and so these are great opportunities for that sort of connection to happen.”

When the rains come, these verdant plots take on another role as an infrastructural asset. As the planet heats up, rainfall increases because a warmer atmosphere can hold more moisture. In response, cities like Los Angeles and Pittsburgh are getting rid of concrete to open up more green spaces, which absorb rainfall, allowing it to seep underground. This reduces pressure on sewer systems that are struggling to handle increasingly heavy deluges. These systems, after all, were designed long ago for a different climate than we’re dealing with today.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

When a city prioritizes green spaces, you can actually hear the difference. Barcelona, for instance, has been developing superblocks, which aim to improve city life by transforming car infrastructure into walkable spaces. That includes the development of “green axes” (the plural of “axis,” not the tool for chopping) full of vegetation and paths for strolling. A recent study found that after these spaces were pedestrianized and vehicles disappeared, average noise levels fell by 3.1 decibels. (For context, hearing a car traveling at 65 mph from 25 feet away would be 77 decibels.)

While 3.1 may not seem like much, each increase of 10 decibels means a tenfold rise in loudness. And we have to consider not just the decibels but how the kind of noise changed as Barcelona developed green axes: Revving engines, honking horns, and even the occasional cacophony of a car accident were replaced with voices. As the built environment dramatically changed, so too did the way that folks on foot experienced their surroundings. “If people see green in general, the noise perception tends to change,” said Samuel Nello-Deakin, a postdoctoral researcher at the Autonomous University of Barcelona and lead author of the study. “You think that things are not as noisy as they actually are. So there’s also this interesting interaction, right, between sort of what you hear and what you see.” In addition, green spaces absorb city racket, keeping it from bouncing off of and between buildings and pavement, insulating residents from the din.

With less commotion come still more gains to public health. Noise pollution is an invisible crisis worldwide, as studies link the stress it causes not just to struggles with mental health, but physical problems like hypertension and heart disease. By contrast, pocket parks and other green spaces encourage people to ditch their cars and move their bodies. “There are also physical health benefits from walking, biking, and being outside that over a lifetime tend to have a cumulative positive effect on what our society spends in health care,” Galipo said.

So as cities increasingly realize and utilize the power of greenery, the environmental, auditory, and social fabric of the urban landscape transforms. “There’s a gravity to this green space that brings people out,” Lambe said. “And all of a sudden, neighbors are connecting, generations are connecting, cultures are connecting. Trees are about the one thing that everybody can agree on.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes the positive real-world impact of pocket gardens in urban areas, highlighting their benefits for mental health, climate resilience, biodiversity, air quality, noise reduction, and community connection. The story is focused on a single topic with substantial context and details, and the impact is broad and meaningful for urban populations worldwide.
articles.personality_title: Pocket gardens bring big benefits to city life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cities are mostly made of concrete, which can make them hot, noisy, and stressful places to live. Small green spaces, like pocket gardens, are being created in busy urban areas to help solve these problems.

**What happened** – Pocket gardens are tiny patches of plants and trees placed in places people walk by, such as sidewalks and hospital grounds. These gardens use native plants that need less water and attract local insects and birds. Cities like Los Angeles and Pittsburgh are adding more pocket gardens to cool the air, reduce flooding, and clean pollution. Barcelona has created green walking paths that lower noise and encourage people to spend time outside.

**Impact** – Pocket gardens help in many ways: they cool down hot city streets, clean the air, reduce noisy traffic sounds, and give people peaceful places to relax and meet others. These gardens also help prevent floods by soaking up rainwater. By using native plants, they support local wildlife and cost less to maintain. This makes cities healthier and more enjoyable.

**What's next step** – More cities are planning to add pocket gardens and green spaces. This will help them handle climate changes like hotter summers and heavier rains. Urban planners will keep designing these spaces to include a variety of plants and places for people to gather. As pocket gardens grow, they may inspire more green ideas to improve city life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Tiny pocket gardens in cities bring cooler air, cleaner streets, less noise, and better places for people and nature to thrive together.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les jardins de poche apportent de grands bienfaits à la vie urbaine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les villes sont principalement faites de béton, ce qui peut les rendre chaudes, bruyantes et stressantes. De petits espaces verts, appelés jardins de poche, sont créés dans les zones urbaines pour aider à résoudre ces problèmes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Les jardins de poche sont de petites parcelles de plantes et d'arbres placées dans des endroits accessibles aux piétons, comme les trottoirs ou les terrains d'hôpitaux. Ces jardins utilisent des plantes locales qui demandent moins d'eau et attirent les insectes et oiseaux du coin. Des villes comme Los Angeles et Pittsburgh ajoutent ces jardins pour rafraîchir l'air, réduire les inondations et purifier la pollution. Barcelone a créé des chemins verts qui diminuent le bruit et encouragent les gens à sortir.

**Impact** – Les jardins de poche aident de plusieurs façons : ils rafraîchissent les rues chaudes, purifient l'air, réduisent le bruit de la circulation et offrent des lieux calmes pour se détendre et se rencontrer. Ils aident aussi à prévenir les inondations en absorbant la pluie. En utilisant des plantes locales, ils soutiennent la faune et coûtent moins cher à entretenir. Cela rend les villes plus saines et agréables.

**Prochaine étape** – De plus en plus de villes prévoient d'ajouter des jardins de poche et des espaces verts. Cela les aidera à faire face aux changements climatiques comme les étés plus chauds et les pluies plus fortes. Les urbanistes continueront à concevoir ces espaces avec une variété de plantes et des endroits pour que les gens se retrouvent. Ces jardins pourraient inspirer d'autres idées vertes pour améliorer la vie urbaine.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les petits jardins de poche en ville apportent de l'air plus frais, des rues plus propres, moins de bruit et de meilleurs endroits pour que les gens et la nature vivent ensemble.
articles.personality_title_es: Los jardines de bolsillo aportan grandes beneficios a la vida urbana
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las ciudades están hechas principalmente de cemento, lo que puede hacerlas calurosas, ruidosas y estresantes. Se están creando pequeños espacios verdes, llamados jardines de bolsillo, en áreas urbanas para ayudar a resolver estos problemas.

**Qué pasó** – Los jardines de bolsillo son pequeñas áreas con plantas y árboles colocadas en lugares por donde caminan las personas, como aceras y terrenos de hospitales. Usan plantas nativas que necesitan menos agua y atraen insectos y aves locales. Ciudades como Los Ángeles y Pittsburgh están agregando estos jardines para enfriar el aire, reducir inundaciones y limpiar la contaminación. Barcelona creó caminos verdes que bajan el ruido y animan a la gente a salir.

**Impacto** – Los jardines de bolsillo ayudan de muchas formas: enfrían las calles calientes, limpian el aire, reducen el ruido del tráfico y ofrecen lugares tranquilos para relajarse y encontrarse. También ayudan a prevenir inundaciones al absorber la lluvia. Al usar plantas nativas, apoyan a la fauna local y cuestan menos mantenimiento. Esto hace que las ciudades sean más saludables y agradables.

**Próximo paso** – Más ciudades planean añadir jardines de bolsillo y espacios verdes. Esto les ayudará a manejar cambios climáticos como veranos más calurosos y lluvias más fuertes. Los planificadores urbanos seguirán diseñando estos espacios con variedad de plantas y lugares para que la gente se reúna. Estos jardines podrían inspirar más ideas verdes para mejorar la vida en la ciudad.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los pequeños jardines de bolsillo en las ciudades traen aire más fresco, calles más limpias, menos ruido y mejores lugares para que las personas y la naturaleza prosperen juntos.
articles.image_prompt: A serene urban corner transformed into a lush pocket garden where diverse native plants form a vibrant canopy over scattered benches, while abstract silhouettes of community connection—interlocking hands made of intertwining roots and branches—rise gently above, symbolizing the harmony of nature, health, and social unity amid the cityscape.

articles.title: Plug-in solar is coming – how dangerous is it and is it worth it?
articles.summary: Plug-in solar panels are a cheaper, simpler alternative to professionally installed panels. But can they really reduce energy bills and are they safe? Matthew Sparkes investigates
articles.content:
Plug-in solar panels are a cheaper, simpler alternative to professionally installed panels. But can they really reduce energy bills and are they safe? Matthew Sparkes investigates

Plug-in solar panels can easily be installed on balconies imageBROKER.com / Alamy Stock Photo

The global surge in solar power is nothing short of extraordinary. Over the last 15 years, the cost of installing a solar system has dropped by 90 per cent and it now accounts for over 80 per cent of the world’s new electricity capacity each year. So when oil and gas prices soared as a result of the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, solar was the obvious place to look for relief for many countries.

But in the UK, it wasn’t just a case of advocating for more of the same – the UK government has said that it will legalise a currently illegal form of solar. So-called plug-in kits will be available “within months” from high-street shops and supermarkets.

These kits are DIY in nature, you simply bring home some panels, place them in a sunny spot and plug them in. There’s no cost of installation and you can start using the sun’s energy to power your home immediately. If you move, just pack up your panels and bring them with you. Solar energy seemingly made even cheaper and available to even more people.

Many countries have already taken to plug-in solar and there are reasons to be excited about it on a global scale but can it really help alleviate energy price rises? How cheap is it? And is it actually safe?

Despite the rapid decreases in cost, installing a traditional solar system isn’t cheap. For an average UK home, estimates for a 4 kilowatt system to cover most energy needs is around £7000. In the US, the average home uses roughly double the energy and the cost of installing a solar system to cover it is around $20,000. These costs include having the panels professionally mounted and a registered electrician installing the system and making alterations to the electricity meter so that excess power can be sold back to the grid – lowering bills or perhaps even generating profit.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Weekly The best of New Scientist, including long-reads, culture, podcasts and news, each week. Sign up to newsletter

Plug-in solar is a simpler proposition. The kits are smaller than a full-scale install, so you might expect to purchase an 800-watt system for around £400 and hope for it to cover something like 20 per cent of an average UK home’s energy needs. Installation is free because it is nothing more than tying the panel to balcony railings, a garden fence or a garage roof and plugging a cable into a wall socket. Once you’re plugged in, you can start using any energy that is generated.

With plug-in solar, excess energy ends up back in the grid but without a professional installation you can’t earn money from it. “Ultimately that energy just gets used by the next-door neighbour,” says Mark Golding at UK solar panel installer Spirit Energy.

Plug-in solar is already an established technology outside the UK. More than a million plug-in solar systems were registered in Germany as of July last year, for instance. Estimates suggest that they cumulatively have capacity of between 1.6 and 2.4 gigawatts there – enough to simultaneously boil half a million kettles.

Germany is the only country attempting to track plug-in solar in any meaningful way, so statistics are hard to come by. But one estimate says that there could be as many as five million kits in use across Europe. Plug-in solar is only a small fraction of the overall energy mix, but for individuals they could take the sting out of bills and cumulatively boost a country’s renewable-generation ability.

Jan Rosenow at the University of Oxford says that uptake could soar if governments keep legislating to allow people to install their own panels. “While individual systems are small, their aggregate impact is becoming meaningful, both in terms of distributed generation and consumer engagement in the energy transition,” says Rosenow.

Plug-in panels are mostly outlawed in the US at present, but Utah became the first state to legalise them last year and many states have similar legislation in the works. Cora Stryker at US pro-solar non-profit Bright Saver says that outside of Utah people have to go through the same amount of admin to install a few solar panels at home as somebody would to construct a 20-megawatt solar farm – a situation that she says is “patently ridiculous”.

Stryker hopes that plug-in solar can alleviate financial hardship, help slow climate change and act as the thin end of the wedge to bring the US up to speed on renewable power. “This is the watershed moment, the tipping point toward a world where the dirt-cheap cost of renewables is actually passed on to the consumer,” she says. Bright Saver estimates that 24 million US households will use a plug-in solar system by 2035.

How safe is it?

But, despite the already widespread use, there is worry among some experts about the safety of plug-in kits. Mark Coles, the head of technical regulations at the Institution of Engineering and Technology (IET), recommends that before anyone buys a plug-in solar kit they have the wiring in their house checked for safety first. And, even after that, the organisation has identified areas of concern.

One issue surrounds residual current devices (RCDs), the safety devices found in fuse boxes that sense when current is leaking to ground – a sign of electrocution or a short circuit – and almost instantaneously cut power. Most RCDs used in the UK aren’t suitable for current flowing in both directions and so could malfunction. In the US, the set-up is different but there are similar problems. One reason why Germany has managed to move so quickly is that by coincidence it standardised bi-directional RCDs in the 1980s.

Another worry of the IET’s is what happens if there are multiple kits and a power cut. In theory the plug-in kits should also shut down in order to prevent “islanding” where one house’s power stays live. But if they’re still generating power then they could deceive each other into thinking the grid is live and keep running. The problem then is that power can jump past the fuse box and electrocute maintenance workers in the area fixing the outage.

“That’s putting those people in danger,” says Coles. “It kind of goes against the concept of ‘just buy this and plug it in’, but in reality we are concerned that there’s a public safety risk here.”

Coles agrees that plug-in solar could bring enormous benefits but wants to ensure manufacturers can prove their systems will behave safely, even in unusual scenarios.

New Scientist put the IET’s safety concerns to the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero and a spokesperson said: “Our tests have shown plug-in solar is safe to use on UK domestic circuits. All products will need to meet UK product safety standards, and we have commissioned an independent study to inform further regulations ahead of their sale.”

Stryker says that, given the catastrophic impact of climate change and soaring energy costs placing many into fuel poverty, the greatest risk to consumers is inaction. She argues that people will adopt technologies like this regardless of whether they’re officially sanctioned and regulated, so the pragmatic approach is to help people do it as safely as possible. “Solar is the cheapest energy on the planet, full stop. It’s actually the cheapest energy humanity’s ever produced,” she says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the imminent legalization and adoption of plug-in solar panels in the UK and other countries, highlighting their potential to make solar energy more affordable and accessible to the general public. This development can significantly reduce energy bills, promote renewable energy adoption, and help alleviate fuel poverty and climate change impacts. The article provides detailed context on costs, safety concerns, and global adoption, making it a substantive and meaningful positive environmental story.
articles.personality_title: Plug-in solar panels set to make clean energy cheaper and easier in the UK
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Solar power has become much cheaper over the last 15 years and now makes up most of the new electricity sources worldwide. Traditional solar panels are still expensive and need professional installation, which can cost thousands of pounds or dollars.

**What happened** – The UK government plans to legalize plug-in solar panels soon. These are small, easy-to-install kits that people can buy from shops, place in sunny spots like balconies, and plug directly into a home socket. This lets people start using solar power without paying for installation. Other countries like Germany already use millions of these kits.

**Impact** – Plug-in solar panels cost much less than full solar systems, around £400 for a smaller kit that can cover part of a home’s electricity use. They can help reduce energy bills and make solar power available to more people, especially those who can’t afford big solar setups. While these panels don’t let users sell extra power back to the grid, the energy they produce still helps neighbors and the environment. Safety experts warn about some risks, but governments and groups are working to ensure the kits are safe for everyday use.

**What’s next step** – The UK will soon allow plug-in solar panels to be sold legally, with safety checks and regulations to follow. Other countries, including parts of the US, are also moving toward legalizing and supporting these systems. This could lead to millions of homes using plug-in solar, helping reduce energy costs and boost clean energy use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Plug-in solar panels offer an affordable and simple way for many people to use renewable energy safely and reduce their electricity bills.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les panneaux solaires plug-in bientôt disponibles au Royaume-Uni pour une énergie propre et abordable
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le coût de l’énergie solaire a beaucoup baissé ces 15 dernières années et représente aujourd’hui la majorité des nouvelles sources d’électricité dans le monde. Les systèmes solaires traditionnels restent chers et nécessitent une installation professionnelle, qui coûte plusieurs milliers d’euros.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le gouvernement britannique prévoit de légaliser bientôt les panneaux solaires plug-in. Ce sont des kits petits et faciles à installer que les gens peuvent acheter en magasin, placer dans un endroit ensoleillé comme un balcon, et brancher directement sur une prise électrique. Cela permet de commencer à utiliser l’énergie solaire sans frais d’installation. D’autres pays comme l’Allemagne utilisent déjà des millions de ces kits.

**Impact** – Les panneaux solaires plug-in coûtent beaucoup moins cher qu’un système complet, environ 400 £ pour un kit plus petit qui peut couvrir une partie de la consommation électrique d’une maison. Ils peuvent aider à réduire les factures d’électricité et rendre l’énergie solaire accessible à plus de personnes, notamment celles qui ne peuvent pas se permettre une grande installation. Même si ces panneaux ne permettent pas de vendre l’électricité excédentaire au réseau, l’énergie produite profite quand même aux voisins et à l’environnement. Des experts en sécurité signalent quelques risques, mais des mesures sont prises pour garantir que les kits soient sûrs.

**Prochaine étape** – Le Royaume-Uni va bientôt autoriser légalement la vente des panneaux solaires plug-in, avec des contrôles de sécurité et des réglementations à venir. D’autres pays, dont certains états américains, avancent aussi vers la légalisation et le soutien de ces systèmes. Cela pourrait conduire à des millions de foyers équipés, aidant à réduire les coûts d’énergie et à augmenter l’utilisation d’énergies renouvelables.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les panneaux solaires plug-in offrent une manière simple et abordable pour beaucoup de personnes d’utiliser l’énergie renouvelable en toute sécurité et de réduire leurs factures d’électricité.
articles.personality_title_es: Los paneles solares plug-in llegarán al Reino Unido para una energía limpia y accesible
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El costo de la energía solar ha bajado mucho en los últimos 15 años y ahora representa la mayoría de las nuevas fuentes de electricidad en el mundo. Los sistemas solares tradicionales siguen siendo caros y necesitan instalación profesional, que cuesta miles de libras o dólares.

**Qué pasó** – El gobierno del Reino Unido planea legalizar pronto los paneles solares plug-in. Estos son kits pequeños y fáciles de instalar que las personas pueden comprar en tiendas, colocar en lugares soleados como balcones y conectar directamente a un enchufe en casa. Esto permite usar energía solar sin pagar por instalación. Otros países como Alemania ya usan millones de estos kits.

**Impacto** – Los paneles solares plug-in cuestan mucho menos que un sistema completo, alrededor de £400 por un kit pequeño que puede cubrir parte del consumo eléctrico de una casa. Pueden ayudar a reducir las facturas de electricidad y hacer que la energía solar sea accesible para más personas, especialmente quienes no pueden pagar una instalación grande. Aunque estos paneles no permiten vender energía extra a la red, la energía que producen ayuda a los vecinos y al medio ambiente. Expertos en seguridad advierten sobre algunos riesgos, pero gobiernos y grupos trabajan para que los kits sean seguros.

**Próximo paso** – El Reino Unido permitirá pronto la venta legal de paneles solares plug-in, con revisiones de seguridad y regulaciones. Otros países, incluidos algunos estados de EE.UU., también avanzan hacia la legalización y apoyo a estos sistemas. Esto podría llevar a millones de hogares usando paneles plug-in, ayudando a bajar costos y aumentar energía limpia.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los paneles solares plug-in ofrecen una forma económica y sencilla para que muchas personas usen energía renovable de manera segura y reduzcan sus facturas eléctricas.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy balcony scene at golden hour featuring a neatly mounted plug-in solar panel gently glowing with soft light, connected by a visible cable to a warm-lit home interior, surrounded by subtle silhouettes of nearby houses linked by faint, flowing lines symbolizing shared energy, all rendered in warm, natural tones with a detailed, painterly style.

articles.title: Scientists discover bizarre termite that looks like a tiny sperm whale
articles.summary: High in a South American rainforest canopy, scientists have discovered a bizarre new termite species that looks strikingly like a miniature sperm whale. Named Cryptotermes mobydicki, this tiny insect has an elongated head and concealed mandibles that give it an uncanny resemblance to the iconic marine giant. Researchers were so surprised by its unusual appearance that they initially thought it belonged to an entirely new genus.
articles.content:
High in the treetops of a South American rainforest, scientists have identified a tiny soldier termite with a surprisingly whale-like appearance. The unusual insect caught the attention of an international team of researchers, who were struck by how different it looked from any known species.

Named Cryptotermes mobydicki, the termite was described by an international research team led by a University of Florida scientist. It features a long, rounded head and mandibles that are mostly hidden from view. Its shape closely resembles a sperm whale, the famous marine animal from Herman Melville's novel, which inspired its name.

"This termite is unlike anything we've ever seen," said Rudolf Scheffrahn, professor of entomology at the UF Institute of Food and Agricultural Sciences (UF/IFAS).

Whale-Like Head Shape Surprises Scientists

According to Scheffrahn, the insect's appearance was so unusual that researchers initially believed they might be looking at an entirely new genus. His taxonomic research is based at the UF/IFAS Fort Lauderdale Research and Education Center.

"The lateral view of the soldier's frontal prominence and elongated head resembles the head of a sperm whale, and in both organisms, the mandibles are eclipsed by the head," he said. "The whale's eye and soldier's antennal socket are comparatively positioned. After I noticed the resemblance to a sperm whale, my coauthors thought the name to be appropriate and whimsical, much like 'ghost orchid' or 'Dumbo octopus.'"

New Species Adds to Termite Evolution Puzzle

This discovery brings the number of known Cryptotermes species in South America to 16. Genetic analysis shows that Cryptotermes mobydicki is closely related to other species found across the Neotropics, including populations in Colombia, Trinidad and the Dominican Republic. These findings provide new insight into how this group of termites has evolved and spread.

The colony was located inside a dead tree standing about eight meters above the forest floor. Its unusual body structure highlights just how diverse termite species can be and points to how many organisms in tropical environments remain undocumented.

Biodiversity Gains and No Threat to Homes

"The discovery of this distinctive new termite species underscores the vast number of unnamed organisms yet to be discovered on our planet," said Scheffrahn.

Each new species adds to scientists' understanding of biodiversity, especially in groups like termites, which include only about 3,000 known species worldwide.

There is also reassuring news for Florida property owners. As a drywood termite, Cryptotermes mobydicki does not pose a risk to buildings or trade. Unlike invasive termites that damage structures in parts of the southeastern United States, this species is limited to its native rainforest habitat and does not spread beyond it.
articles.reasoning: The article reports the discovery of a new termite species with unique features, contributing to biodiversity knowledge and evolutionary science. This has broad scientific significance and highlights the richness of tropical ecosystems, which is inspiring and hopeful for conservation and understanding of life on Earth.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover termite that looks like a tiny sperm whale
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists exploring high in the South American rainforest canopy found a new termite species. Termites are small insects that usually live in wood and soil, and this discovery adds to what we know about the diversity of life in tropical forests.

**What happened** – Researchers led by a scientist from the University of Florida found a tiny soldier termite with a long, rounded head and hidden jaws. This unusual shape made the termite look very much like a miniature sperm whale, a large sea animal. They named it Cryptotermes mobydicki after the famous whale in literature. At first, scientists thought it might be a completely new group of termites because of its strange appearance.

**Impact** – This termite is special because it shows how different and surprising nature can be, even in small insects. It helps scientists understand how termite species have changed and spread across the Americas. Finding this new species also reminds us there are many living things still unknown in tropical forests. Importantly, this termite does not harm buildings or homes, so it is not a threat to people.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will study Cryptotermes mobydicki further to learn more about how termites evolved. They will also keep searching in tropical forests to discover other unknown species. This helps build a clearer picture of biodiversity and how ecosystems work.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A newly discovered termite that looks like a tiny sperm whale adds to our understanding of insect diversity and evolution in tropical rainforests.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent une termite qui ressemble à un petit cachalot
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques explorant la canopée d’une forêt tropicale d’Amérique du Sud ont découvert une nouvelle espèce de termite. Les termites sont de petits insectes vivant généralement dans le bois ou le sol, et cette découverte enrichit nos connaissances sur la diversité de la vie dans les forêts tropicales.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs dirigée par un scientifique de l’Université de Floride a trouvé une termite soldat minuscule avec une tête longue et arrondie et des mandibules cachées. Cette forme inhabituelle fait ressembler la termite à un petit cachalot, un grand animal marin. Ils l’ont nommée Cryptotermes mobydicki, en référence au célèbre cachalot de la littérature. Au début, les chercheurs pensaient qu’il s’agissait d’un tout nouveau groupe de termites à cause de son apparence étrange.

**Impact** – Cette termite est spéciale car elle montre à quel point la nature peut être différente et surprenante, même chez de petits insectes. Elle aide les scientifiques à comprendre comment les termites ont évolué et se sont répandus en Amérique. Cette découverte rappelle aussi qu’il existe encore beaucoup d’espèces inconnues dans les forêts tropicales. De plus, cette termite ne menace pas les maisons, donc elle ne pose pas de risque pour les humains.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier davantage Cryptotermes mobydicki pour mieux comprendre l’évolution des termites. Ils continueront aussi à chercher d’autres espèces inconnues dans les forêts tropicales. Cela permettra de mieux connaître la biodiversité et le fonctionnement des écosystèmes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle termite ressemblant à un petit cachalot enrichit notre compréhension de la diversité et de l’évolution des insectes dans les forêts tropicales.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren termita que parece una pequeña ballena esperma
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Científicos que exploraban la copa de un bosque tropical en Sudamérica encontraron una nueva especie de termita. Las termitas son insectos pequeños que suelen vivir en madera y suelo, y este hallazgo amplía nuestro conocimiento sobre la diversidad de la vida en bosques tropicales.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de investigadores liderado por un científico de la Universidad de Florida encontró una termita soldado muy pequeña con una cabeza larga y redondeada y mandíbulas ocultas. Esta forma inusual hizo que la termita pareciera una pequeña ballena esperma, un gran animal marino. La llamaron Cryptotermes mobydicki, en honor a la famosa ballena de la literatura. Al principio, los científicos pensaron que era un grupo completamente nuevo de termitas debido a su extraña apariencia.

**Impacto** – Esta termita es especial porque muestra lo diferente y sorprendente que puede ser la naturaleza, incluso en insectos pequeños. Ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo las especies de termitas han evolucionado y se han extendido por América. El descubrimiento también recuerda que hay muchas especies aún desconocidas en los bosques tropicales. Además, esta termita no daña casas, por lo que no representa una amenaza para las personas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos estudiarán más a fondo a Cryptotermes mobydicki para aprender más sobre la evolución de las termitas. También seguirán buscando otras especies desconocidas en los bosques tropicales. Esto ayudará a tener una imagen más clara de la biodiversidad y cómo funcionan los ecosistemas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una termita recién descubierta que parece una pequeña ballena esperma amplía nuestro conocimiento sobre la diversidad y evolución de insectos en los bosques tropicales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a tiny termite with a long, rounded head and hidden mandibles perched on a slender branch high in a lush South American rainforest canopy, its head subtly shaped and shaded to evoke the silhouette of a sperm whale, surrounded by soft natural greens and browns that emphasize the unique blend of insect and marine mammal forms in a harmonious, peaceful environment.
